Corner is correct. I've made a PR to change it.

### Observed behavior

```
import pandas as pd
import random
from bokeh.plotting import hold, figure, ColumnDataSource, scatter, curplot, show, line, output_file
from collections import OrderedDict   
from bokeh.objects import HoverTool

dates = pd.date_range('2014/09/01 00:00', freq='H', periods=24, tz='Europe/Berlin')
data = pd.DataFrame(rand(48).reshape(24,2), dates, columns=['a', 'b'])

data['index'] = data.index       
data['index_str'] = [i.strftime('%Y/%m/%d %H:%M') for i in data.index]


hold()
output_file("test.html")
figure(x_axis_type="datetime", tools='hover') 

r = lambda: random.randint(20,235) 
for  col in data.columns[:-2]:
    c_data = data[['index', 'index_str', col]]
    c_data.columns = ['index', 'index_str', 'value']            
    source = ColumnDataSource(dict(c_data))    

    color = ('#%02X%02X%02X' % (r(),r(),r()))                      
    scatter('index', 'value', source=source, legend=col, color=color)
    line('index', 'value', source=source, legend=col, color=color)

# Built the hover      
hover = [t for t in curplot().tools if isinstance(t, HoverTool)][0]        
hover.tooltips = OrderedDict([
    ('index', '@index_str'),
    ('value', '@value')])


show()        

```

_No response_

### Expected behavior

* create a dialog, set visible=False
* display a dialog via callback that sets visible=True
* hide a dialog via callback that sets visible=False
* display a dialog again via callback that sets visible=True

Dialog should be displayed.

### Observed behavior

Dialog is not displayed the second time around.

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.models import Button, Dialog, CustomJS
from bokeh.plotting import show
from bokeh.layouts import row

open_button = Button(label="Open Dialog", width=200)

close_button = Button(label="Close", width=100)

dialog = Dialog(
    title="Example Dialog",
    content=close_button,
    visible=False
)

open_button.js_on_click(CustomJS(
    args=dict(dialog=dialog),
    code="""
    console.log('open button clicked');
    dialog.visible = true;
    """
))

close_button.js_on_click(CustomJS(
    args=dict(dialog=dialog),
    code="""
    console.log('close button clicked');
    dialog.visible = false;
    """
))

layout = row(open_button, dialog)
show(layout)
```

### Stack traceback or browser console output

Console log shows that callbacks are propagated correctly.

Expected behavior

Hi there,

I'm trying to apply a customized JS hover formatter to the name field with the `*_stack` glyphs, and I can't seem to figure it out. Notice that the same functionality works well for the x axis.

### Observed behavior

In the minimal example's screenshot, notice the "Value" tooltip; it's not using the custom formatter and is oddly printing "4th". However, the fruit tooltip seems to work just fine and shows the expected "Hello Grapes!" value.

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.io import show
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, HoverTool, CustomJSHover
from bokeh.plotting import figure

fruits = ['Apples', 'Pears', 'Nectarines', 'Plums', 'Grapes', 'Strawberries']
years = ["2015", "2016", "2017"]
colors = ["#c9d9d3", "#718dbf", "#e84d60"]

data = {'fruits' : fruits,
        '2015'   : [2, 1, 4, 3, 2, 4],
        '2016'   : [5, 3, 4, 2, 4, 6],
        '2017'   : [3, 2, 4, 4, 5, 3]}

source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)

hoverfrmtr = CustomJSHover(code='return "Hello " + value + "!";')
hovertool = HoverTool(
    tooltips=[('Year', '$name'),
        ('Fruit', "@fruits{custom}"),
        ('Value', "@$name{custom}")],
    formatters={
        "@fruits": hoverfrmtr, "@$name": hoverfrmtr,
        **{f'@{year}': hoverfrmtr for year in years}})

p = figure(x_range=fruits, height=350, title="Fruit Counts by Year",
           toolbar_location=None, tools=[hovertool])

renderers = p.vbar_stack(years, x='fruits', width=0.9, color=colors,
    source=source, legend_label=years, name=years)

show(p)
```

Expected behavior

Hi there,

I'm trying to apply a customized JS hover formatter to the name field with the `*_stack` glyphs, and I can't seem to figure it out. Notice that the same functionality works well for the x axis.

### Observed behavior

In the minimal example's screenshot, notice the "Value" tooltip; it's not using the custom formatter and is oddly printing "4th". However, the fruit tooltip seems to work just fine and shows the expected "Hello Grapes!" value.

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.io import show
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, HoverTool, CustomJSHover
from bokeh.plotting import figure

fruits = ['Apples', 'Pears', 'Nectarines', 'Plums', 'Grapes', 'Strawberries']
years = ["2015", "2016", "2017"]
colors = ["#c9d9d3", "#718dbf", "#e84d60"]

data = {'fruits' : fruits,
        '2015'   : [2, 1, 4, 3, 2, 4],
        '2016'   : [5, 3, 4, 2, 4, 6],
        '2017'   : [3, 2, 4, 4, 5, 3]}

source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)

hoverfrmtr = CustomJSHover(code='return "Hello " + value + "!";')
hovertool = HoverTool(
    tooltips=[('Year', '$name'),
        ('Fruit', "@fruits{custom}"),
        ('Value', "@$name{custom}")],
    formatters={
        "@fruits": hoverfrmtr, "@$name": hoverfrmtr,
        **{f'@{year}': hoverfrmtr for year in years}})

p = figure(x_range=fruits, height=350, title="Fruit Counts by Year",
           toolbar_location=None, tools=[hovertool])

renderers = p.vbar_stack(years, x='fruits', width=0.9, color=colors,
    source=source, legend_label=years, name=years)

show(p)
```

here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this Software versions Python version : 3.12.8 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | (main, Dec 11 2024, 16:48:34) [MSC v.1929 64 bit (AMD64)] IPython version : 8.27.0 Tornado version : 6.4.2 NumPy version : 1.26.4 Bokeh version : 3.6.0 BokehJS static path : C:\ProgramData\anaconda3\envs\BOKEH\Lib\site-packages\bokeh\server\static node.js version : (not installed) npm version : (not installed) jupyter_bokeh version : 4.0.5 Operating system : Windows-11-10.0.26100-SP0 ### Browser name and version _No response_ ### Jupyter notebook / Jupyter Lab version _No response_ ### Expected behavior I have added a ScaleBar to a figure with geographical data and it seems that the default ScaleBar length is not correct. The distance I calculate with the bar length is not the one I find with other methods (Google Maps for example). ### Observed behavior For the minimal reproductible example below (run in a jupyter notebook), the distance found when calculating the distance with the scale (using cross product (distance_between_two_points_cm * scale_distance_m)/scale_bar_length_cm) is 57m whereas I find 38m with other tools such as Geoportail, Google Maps or `geopy.distance.geodesic()` The minimal reproductible example is a sort of converter, I measured the distances in centimers with a ruler on my screen... ### Example code ```Python import panel as pn pn.extension(sizing_mode='scale_both') from bokeh.plotting import figure from bokeh.models import ScaleBar from bokeh.io import output_notebook, curdoc curdoc().clear() output_notebook() from bokeh.models import WMTSTileSource from holoviews.util.transform import lon_lat_to_easting_northing from geopy.distance import geodesic from math import sin, cos, sqrt, atan2, radians from bokeh.util.info import print_info print_info() rue_copernic_28 = (2.2885813, 48.869313) rue_copernic_32 = (2.288077, 48.869387) def create_figure(): # position de la charge, zone d'étude et limites d'étude en web mercator x_rue_copernic_28, y_rue_copernic_28 = lon_lat_to_easting_northing(*rue_copernic_28) x_rue_copernic_32, y_rue_copernic_32 = lon_lat_to_easting_northing(*rue_copernic_32) # création de la figure avec les coordoonées en mercator fig = figure(x_axis_type='mercator', y_axis_type='mercator', tools=['pan', 'box_zoom', 'wheel_zoom', 'save', 'reset'], active_drag='pan', active_scroll='wheel_zoom', title="Test échelles") # utilisation du serveur en ligne de images tuilées tiles = WMTSTileSource(url="https://data.geopf.fr/tms/1.0.0/GEOGRAPHICALGRIDSYSTEMS.PLANIGNV2/{Z}/{X}/{Y}.png", name='PLAN IGN', attribution=""" © Institut national de l'information géographique et forestière """, max_zoom=19) # ajout du fond de carte sur la figure fig.add_tile(tiles) # point 1 fig.scatter(x_rue_copernic_28, y_rue_copernic_28, size=18, color='red', marker='circle_cross', line_color='black', legend_label='28 Rue Copernic') # point 2 fig.scatter(x_rue_copernic_32, y_rue_copernic_32, size=18, color='green', marker='circle_cross', line_color='black', legend_label='32 Rue Copernic') # ajouter l'échelle seulement après avoir défini les limites fig.add_layout(ScaleBar()) # mettre les légendes en bas à droite fig.legend.location = 'bottom_right' return fig def calculer_distance_en_m(echelle_m, echelle_cm, mesure_cm): return echelle_m * mesure_cm/echelle_cm def haversine_distance(point1, point2): # source : https://andrew.hedges.name/experiments/haversine/ # Approximate radius of earth in m R = 6373.0*1e3 lat1 = radians(point1[1]) lon1 = radians(point1[0]) lat2 = radians(point2[1]) lon2 = radians(point2[0]) dlon = lon2 - lon1 dlat = lat2 - lat1 a = sin(dlat / 2)**2 + cos(lat1) * cos(lat2) * sin(dlon / 2)**2 c = 2 * atan2(sqrt(a), sqrt(1 - a)) return R * c map_pane = pn.pane.Bokeh(create_figure()) echelle = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Value échelle (m)', value=15) mesure = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Mesure échelle (cm)', value=3.6) mesure_entre_deux_points = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Mesure (cm)', value=14.7) bokeh_distance = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Distance selon échelle (m)', disabled=True, value=pn.bind(calculer_distance_en_m, echelle, mesure, mesure_entre_deux_points)) geopy_distance = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Distance calculée avec Geopy (m)', disabled=True, value=geodesic((rue_copernic_28[1], rue_copernic_28[0]), (rue_copernic_32[1],rue_copernic_32[0])).km*1e3) haversine_distance = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Distance haversine (m)', disabled=True, value=haversine_distance(rue_copernic_28, rue_copernic_32)) layout = pn.Row(map_pane, pn.WidgetBox("### Mesures", echelle, mesure, mesure_entre_deux_points, bokeh_distance, geopy_distance, haversine_distance)) layout ``` ### Stack traceback or browser console output I am not sure how the calculation of Software versions Python version : 3.12.8 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | (main, Dec 11 2024, 16:48:34) [MSC v.1929 64 bit (AMD64)] IPython version : 8.27.0 Tornado version : 6.4.2 NumPy version : 1.26.4 Bokeh version : 3.6.0 BokehJS static path : C:\ProgramData\anaconda3\envs\BOKEH\Lib\site-packages\bokeh\server\static node.js version : (not installed) npm version : (not installed) jupyter_bokeh version : 4.0.5 Operating system : Windows-11-10.0.26100-SP0 ### Browser name and version _No response_ ### Jupyter notebook / Jupyter Lab version _No response_ ### Expected behavior I have added a ScaleBar to a figure with geographical data and it seems that the default ScaleBar length is not correct. The distance I calculate with the bar length is not the one I find with other methods (Google Maps for example). ### Observed behavior For the minimal reproductible example below (run in a jupyter notebook), the distance found when calculating the distance with the scale (using cross product (distance_between_two_points_cm * scale_distance_m)/scale_bar_length_cm) is 57m whereas I find 38m with other tools such as Geoportail, Google Maps or `geopy.distance.geodesic()` The minimal reproductible example is a sort of converter, I measured the distances in centimers with a ruler on my screen... ### Example code ```Python import panel as pn pn.extension(sizing_mode='scale_both') from bokeh.plotting import figure from bokeh.models import ScaleBar from bokeh.io import output_notebook, curdoc curdoc().clear() output_notebook() from bokeh.models import WMTSTileSource from holoviews.util.transform import lon_lat_to_easting_northing from geopy.distance import geodesic from math import sin, cos, sqrt, atan2, radians from bokeh.util.info import print_info print_info() rue_copernic_28 = (2.2885813, 48.869313) rue_copernic_32 = (2.288077, 48.869387) def create_figure(): # position de la charge, zone d'étude et limites d'étude en web mercator x_rue_copernic_28, y_rue_copernic_28 = lon_lat_to_easting_northing(*rue_copernic_28) x_rue_copernic_32, y_rue_copernic_32 = lon_lat_to_easting_northing(*rue_copernic_32) # création de la figure avec les coordoonées en mercator fig = figure(x_axis_type='mercator', y_axis_type='mercator', tools=['pan', 'box_zoom', 'wheel_zoom', 'save', 'reset'], active_drag='pan', active_scroll='wheel_zoom', title="Test échelles") # utilisation du serveur en ligne de images tuilées tiles = WMTSTileSource(url="https://data.geopf.fr/tms/1.0.0/GEOGRAPHICALGRIDSYSTEMS.PLANIGNV2/{Z}/{X}/{Y}.png", name='PLAN IGN', attribution=""" © Institut national de l'information géographique et forestière """, max_zoom=19) # ajout du fond de carte sur la figure fig.add_tile(tiles) # point 1 fig.scatter(x_rue_copernic_28, y_rue_copernic_28, size=18, color='red', marker='circle_cross', line_color='black', legend_label='28 Rue Copernic') # point 2 fig.scatter(x_rue_copernic_32, y_rue_copernic_32, size=18, color='green', marker='circle_cross', line_color='black', legend_label='32 Rue Copernic') # ajouter l'échelle seulement après avoir défini les limites fig.add_layout(ScaleBar()) # mettre les légendes en bas à droite fig.legend.location = 'bottom_right' return fig def calculer_distance_en_m(echelle_m, echelle_cm, mesure_cm): return echelle_m * mesure_cm/echelle_cm def haversine_distance(point1, point2): # source : https://andrew.hedges.name/experiments/haversine/ # Approximate radius of earth in m R = 6373.0*1e3 lat1 = radians(point1[1]) lon1 = radians(point1[0]) lat2 = radians(point2[1]) lon2 = radians(point2[0]) dlon = lon2 - lon1 dlat = lat2 - lat1 a = sin(dlat / 2)**2 + cos(lat1) * cos(lat2) * sin(dlon / 2)**2 c = 2 * atan2(sqrt(a), sqrt(1 - a)) return R * c map_pane = pn.pane.Bokeh(create_figure()) echelle = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Value échelle (m)', value=15) mesure = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Mesure échelle (cm)', value=3.6) mesure_entre_deux_points = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Mesure (cm)', value=14.7) bokeh_distance = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Distance selon échelle (m)', disabled=True, value=pn.bind(calculer_distance_en_m, echelle, mesure, mesure_entre_deux_points)) geopy_distance = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Distance calculée avec Geopy (m)', disabled=True, value=geodesic((rue_copernic_28[1], rue_copernic_28[0]), (rue_copernic_32[1],rue_copernic_32[0])).km*1e3) haversine_distance = pn.widgets.FloatInput(name='Distance haversine (m)', disabled=True, value=haversine_distance(rue_copernic_28, rue_copernic_32)) layout = pn.Row(map_pane, pn.WidgetBox("### Mesures", echelle, mesure, mesure_entre_deux_points, bokeh_distance, geopy_distance, haversine_distance)) layout ``` ### Stack traceback or browser console output I am not sure how the calculation of the bar length is implemented but I found the same incorrect distance with the Geopy method when I unintentionally switched the `(lat, lon)` tuple to `(lon, lat)` as input for `geopy.distance.geodesic()`. ### Screenshots ![Measure with Geoportail](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ae726008-955e-402f-884e-86805098a334) ![Google Maps ### Expected behavior

Using a custom data model should cause any issues when running a bokeh server and refreshing the page.

### Observed behavior

When refreshing the page in the browser (chrome) after a certain time (ca. 30 seconds) the server will crash hard:
error: `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'bokeh_app_5f0445280a0943b48f7b4b629fa8b6d7'`
Example/code is from here: https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/examples/plotting/data_models.html

Note: 
- At first everything works as intended, the issue only appears ca. 30 seconds after launching the bokeh server
- Sometimes more than one refresh is required to trigger the error




### Example code

```Python
import numpy as np

from bokeh.core.properties import Float
from bokeh.io import curdoc
from bokeh.model import DataModel
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, CustomJS
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show

doc = curdoc()

class Params(DataModel):
    amp = Float(default=0.1, help="Amplitude")
    freq = Float(default=0.1, help="Frequency")
    phase = Float(default=0, help="Phase")
    offset = Float(default=-5, help="Offset")

params = Params(amp=2, freq=3, phase=0.4, offset=1)

A = params.amp
k = params.freq
phi = params.phase
B = params.offset
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = A*np.sin(k*x + phi) + B

source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=x, y=y))

plot = figure(tags=[params], y_range=(-10, 10), title="Data models example")
plot.line("x", "y", source=source, line_width=3, line_alpha=0.6)

callback = CustomJS(args=dict(source=source, params=params), code="""
    const data = source.data
    const A = params.amp
    const k = params.freq
    const phi = params.phase
    const B = params.offset

    const x = source.data.x
    const y = Array.from(x, (x) => B + A*Math.sin(k*x+phi))
    source.data = { x, y }
""")

params.js_on_change("amp", callback)
params.js_on_change("freq", callback)
params.js_on_change("phase", callback)
params.js_on_change("offset", callback)

doc.add_root(plot)
```

### Expected behavior

Using a custom data model should cause any issues when running a bokeh server and refreshing the page.

### Observed behavior

When refreshing the page in the browser (chrome) after a certain time (ca. 30 seconds) the server will crash hard:
error: `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'bokeh_app_5f0445280a0943b48f7b4b629fa8b6d7'`
Example/code is from here: https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/examples/plotting/data_models.html

Note: 
- At first everything works as intended, the issue only appears ca. 30 seconds after launching the bokeh server
- Sometimes more than one refresh is required to trigger the error




### Example code

```Python
import numpy as np

from bokeh.core.properties import Float
from bokeh.io import curdoc
from bokeh.model import DataModel
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, CustomJS
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show

doc = curdoc()

class Params(DataModel):
    amp = Float(default=0.1, help="Amplitude")
    freq = Float(default=0.1, help="Frequency")
    phase = Float(default=0, help="Phase")
    offset = Float(default=-5, help="Offset")

params = Params(amp=2, freq=3, phase=0.4, offset=1)

A = params.amp
k = params.freq
phi = params.phase
B = params.offset
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = A*np.sin(k*x + phi) + B

source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=x, y=y))

plot = figure(tags=[params], y_range=(-10, 10), title="Data models example")
plot.line("x", "y", source=source, line_width=3, line_alpha=0.6)

callback = CustomJS(args=dict(source=source, params=params), code="""
    const data = source.data
    const A = params.amp
    const k = params.freq
    const phi = params.phase
    const B = params.offset

    const x = source.data.x
    const y = Array.from(x, (x) => B + A*Math.sin(k*x+phi))
    source.data = { x, y }
""")

params.js_on_change("amp", callback)
params.js_on_change("freq", callback)
params.js_on_change("phase", callback)
params.js_on_change("offset", callback)

doc.add_root(plot)
```

```
    all_objs = objs if objs is not None else HasProps.model_class_reverse_map.values()

    for obj in all_objs:
        if hasattr(obj, "__implementation__"):
            continue
        name = obj.__view_module__.split(".")[0]
        if name == "bokeh":
            continue
        if name in names:
            continue
        names.add(name)
        try:
            module = __import__(name)
```

But I think it is questionable that `HasProps.model_class_reverse_map` would include this module in the first place. @mattpap do you have any quick thoughts whether there is a reason it is included? ### Expected behavior

The Div text should be shown in the exported SVG file and no random temporary files should be created!

### Observed behavior

Exporting Bokeh layout or gridplot to SVG does not show Div elements. Moreover, the code generates a new temporary `html` files (with a random name) in the working directory every time the code is executed even when I have specified an `output_file` name. If `output_file` name is specified, then the temporary file creation is skipped at the first execution. 

### Example code

```Python
import numpy as np

from bokeh.plotting import figure, output_file
from bokeh.models import Div
import bokeh.layouts as layouts
from bokeh.io import export_svg

N = 10000

x = np.random.normal(0, np.pi, N)
y = np.sin(x) + np.random.normal(0, 0.2, N)

TOOLS = "pan,wheel_zoom,box_zoom,reset,save,box_select"

p = figure(tools=TOOLS, output_backend="svg")
p.scatter(x, y, alpha=0.1, nonselection_alpha=0.001)

div = Div(text='Plotting random')

fig = layouts.column(div, p)

output_file('testSVG.html')

export_svg(fig, filename='testSVG.svg')
```

I had imagined in the past that "reconnect" would actually mean generating and refreshing an entirely new session on the page, i.e "automatic reloading". For instance, one common reason for a disconnect would be that a session was closed and deleted due to an activity timeout. In that case, with the original session destroyed, there is nothing to "actually reconnect" to. It seems to me that actually re-connecting to the same session could be very difficult for other reasons as well: 

* In the case of load balancers, how to guarantee that the connection lands on the same identical instance that is hosting the  session? 
* How are problems with inconsistent state to be addressed, e.g. if a connection dropped in the middle of an update.

I'm not actually sure how or if "actual reconnection to the same session" can be made to work, or how difficult it would be. 

If instead, "re-connection" is interpreted to mean "automatic reload" then I think all these questions are sidestepped. That also handles the session timeout case, what we had in mind primarily, which "actual reconnection" would not be able to handle. 

cc @bokeh/dev and @jbednar since this is probably of interest to HV. ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2555926431/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
import pandas as pd
import random
from bokeh.plotting import hold, figure, ColumnDataSource, scatter, curplot, show, line, output_file
from collections import OrderedDict   
from bokeh.objects import HoverTool

dates = pd.date_range('2014/09/01 00:00', freq='H', periods=24, tz='Europe/Berlin')
data = pd.DataFrame(rand(48).reshape(24,2), dates, columns=['a', 'b'])

data['index'] = data.index       
data['index_str'] = [i.strftime('%Y/%m/%d %H:%M') for i in data.index]


hold()
output_file("test.html")
figure(x_axis_type="datetime", tools='hover') 

r = lambda: random.randint(20,235) 
for  col in data.columns[:-2]:
    c_data = data[['index', 'index_str', col]]
    c_data.columns = ['index', 'index_str', 'value']            
    source = ColumnDataSource(dict(c_data))    

    color = ('#%02X%02X%02X' % (r(),r(),r()))                      
    scatter('index', 'value', source=source, legend=col, color=color)
    line('index', 'value', source=source, legend=col, color=color)

# Built the hover      
hover = [t for t in curplot().tools if isinstance(t, HoverTool)][0]        
hover.tooltips = OrderedDict([
    ('index', '@index_str'),
    ('value', '@value')])


show()        

```

### Expected behavior

I am trying add and edit lines. This funtionality works (see code below). According to the online reference (see image below), the ability to snap to vettices is enabled by the use of a vertex renderer.

ISSUE: I cannot get snapping working

### Observed behavior

There is no indication that snap is working. I cannot see any way that it can be controlled - turned on or off, etc. Is it just vertices that are intended to be snapped to? Some sort of tools to control snapping would be useful, and being able to control snap settings via python.

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.io import curdoc
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, PolyEditTool, PolyDrawTool
from bokeh.plotting import figure
from bokeh.layouts import column

# Initial data for two lines
line_data = {
    'xs': [[10, 20], [6, 15]],
    'ys': [[1, 4], [6, 9]],
    'colors': ['blue', 'green']
}

# Create ColumnDataSource
source = ColumnDataSource(data=line_data)

# Create a plot
plot = figure(title="Editable Lines", x_axis_label='X', y_axis_label='Y', width=600, height=500)

# Add the line to the plot using MultiLine
lines = plot.multi_line(xs='xs', ys='ys', source=source, line_width=2, line_color='colors')
#lines = plot.multi_line(xs='xs', ys='ys', source=source, line_width=2)

# Create a vertex renderer for snapping
vr = plot.scatter([], [], size=10, marker='circle_dot', line_color='navy', fill_color='orange', alpha=0.5)

# Add PolyEditTool for editing lines with snapping to vertices
poly_edit_tool = PolyEditTool(renderers=[lines], vertex_renderer=vr)
plot.add_tools(poly_edit_tool)

# Add PolyDrawTool for adding new lines
poly_draw_tool = PolyDrawTool(renderers=[lines], default_overrides = {'colors': 'orange'})
plot.add_tools(poly_draw_tool)

# Layout of the application
layout = column(plot)
```

### Expected behavior

I am trying to get the bokeh plot into an pdf report as an image.
I am running chrome for testing along with my main application in docker.

when I run my python backend via python3 it works and generates images, everything is fine. When I run it in docker I would also expect it to work. But it does not.



### Observed behavior

Currently export_png() crashes the chromedriver and I could not figure out why. Even looked into your code what you are doing over here to maybe contribute something.

I noticed this tinny annoying comment and I am suspecting it could be the cause of my issue:
```
export.py:124     filename = os.fspath(filename) # XXX: Image.save() doesn't fully support PathLike
```
Could this be related?





### Example code

```Python
This is how I setup chrome-for-testing in Dockerfile:

# Download and set up Chrome binary
RUN wget -q https://storage.googleapis.com/chrome-for-testing-public/129.0.6668.70/linux64/chrome-linux64.zip && \
    unzip chrome-linux64.zip \
    && mkdir /usr/bin/google-chrome \
    && mv chrome-linux64/* /usr/bin/google-chrome/ \
    && rm chrome-linux64.zip \
    && cd ..

# Download and set up Chromedriver binary
RUN wget -N https://storage.googleapis.com/chrome-for-testing-public/129.0.6668.70/linux64/chromedriver-linux64.zip \
    && unzip chromedriver-linux64.zip \
    && cd chromedriver-linux64 \
    && mkdir /usr/local/bin/chromedriver \
    && mv chromedriver /usr/local/bin/chromedriver/ \
    && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/chromedriver/chromedriver \
    && cd ..


This is how I use it in python

def generate_plot_image(self, plot, gatewayId: str, plot_type: str) -> str:

        chrome_options = Options()
        chrome_options.add_argument(
            "--no-sandbox"
        )  # Required for some server environments
        chrome_options.add_argument("--headless=new")  # Run in headless mode
        chrome_options.add_argument("--remote-debugging-port=9222")
        chrome_options.add_argument(
            "--disable-dev-shm-usage"
        )  # Overcome limited resource problems

        driver = webdriver.Chrome(
            executable_path="/usr/local/bin/chromedriver/chromedriver",
            options=chrome_options,
        )

        # Create the path
        path = f"/tmp/{gatewayId}/"

        # Create the directory if it doesn't exist
        os.makedirs(path, exist_ok=True)

        filepath = f"{path}/{plot_type}.png"

        export_png(plot, filename=filepath, webdriver=driver)

        return filepath
```

### Expected behavior

I have a plot with datetime x-axis used for displaying monthly data (VBar).
I want to use MonthsTicker to only have ticks and matching labels (short month name and year) on months.



### Observed behavior

I get no ticks nor labels at all,

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.io import show
from bokeh.plotting import figure
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, DatetimeTickFormatter, MonthsTicker
from datetime import datetime

# Sample data for 6 months
dates = [
    datetime(2024, 1, 1), datetime(2024, 2, 1), datetime(2024, 3, 1),
    datetime(2024, 4, 1), datetime(2024, 5, 1), datetime(2024, 6, 1)
]
values = [10, 20, 15, 25, 18, 22]

# Create a ColumnDataSource
source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(dates=dates, values=values))

# Create a figure with a datetime x-axis
p = figure(x_axis_type="datetime", title="Monthly Data", toolbar_location="right", tools="pan,wheel_zoom,box_zoom,reset")

# Add VBars
p.vbar(x='dates', top='values', width=20*24*60*60*1000, source=source, legend_label="Values", color="blue")

# Customize the x-axis with MonthsTicker
p.xaxis.ticker = MonthsTicker()
p.xaxis.formatter = DatetimeTickFormatter(months="%b %Y")

p.xaxis.axis_label = "Month"
p.xaxis.major_label_orientation = 1.0  # Rotate labels for better readability

p.yaxis.axis_label = "Value"

# Show the plot
show(p)
```

### Stack traceback or browser console output

Using the following code does produce tick labels however it can also show ticks between months.
p.xaxis.ticker = DatetimeTicker(desired_num_ticks=6)
p.xaxis.formatter = DatetimeTickFormatter(months="%b %Y")

Software versions

In the user guide of Application > Updating from unlocked callbacks, the sample codes have a bug such that the plot appears to be not working.

https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/user_guide/server/app.html#updating-from-threads

https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/blob/5be848f0e7fa34301cd7b390426328dbf7a78d6c/docs/bokeh/source/docs/user_guide/server/app.rst?plain=1#L528

It is missing `radius`, and should be something like:
```python
l = p.circle(x="x", y="y", radius=0.5, color="color", source=source)
```

Without `radius`, users simply an empty plot.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14139","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14139/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14139/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14139/events","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14139","id":2633105827,"node_id":"I_kwDOADqB3M6c8gGj","number":14139,"title":"[FEATURE] Trimesh ","user":{"login":"gmerritt123","id":58000181,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDAwMTgx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/58000181?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123","html_url":"https://github.com/gmerritt123","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gmerritt123/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":91228996,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTIyODk5Ng==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/type:%20discussion","name":"type: discussion","color":"2659A0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-11-04T15:20:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-05T22:18:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem description

A huge amount of engineering is completed via the finite element method. A finite element "mesh" is a topologically-aware construct consisting of elements and nodes. Most commonly, triangular elements are used for numerical solver reasons. 

Nodes have specified coordinates, and elements are described as the area (or volume) bound by a collection of node indices. e.g.

```
nds = {'x':[0,2,2,3] ,'y':[0,2,0,0]}
els = [(0,1,2),(1,2,3)]  #first element is area bound by node indices 0,1,2 and second element = area bound by node indices 1,2,3
```
The obvious advantage to storing the data this way is that node coordinates do not need to be stored multiple times, and topologic integrity.

I'm currently translating the above kind of data structure into one bokeh can use for a Patches glyph, e.g. the above becomes:

```
data = {'xs':[[0,2,2],[2,2,3]], 'ys':[[0,2,0],[2,0,0]]}
```

And often I run into size limitations (nodes/elements can be into 100s of thousands or even millions for my models):

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/74bc1202-e1c4-4a88-bd1a-f91b276d247f)



### Feature description

Specifically, I would like a means of rendering triangular 2D meshes: a sort of framework that would hold a CDS of node coordinates/nodal properties, and another "special" CDS/data model that holds a regular 2D array (e.g. shape  = (nElements, 3)) describing the node indices comprising each element. The renderers would be comprise 2 glyphs: a Scatter for the nodes, and either Patches or some sort of special/custom Triangle glyph for the elements.

### Potential alternatives

Thinking about this, it seems like a lot of the networkx Graph type work is related to this in that the data structure is very similar. However the key difference is that I want to render the *area bound* by connected nodes and not the connections themselves. So starting from the GraphLayoutProvider side of things might be an easier start/implementation.

### Additional information

Worth mentioning that I'm not sure if generalizing out the TriMesh to a "RegularNMesh" would be viable, where the shape of the element array would determine the element glyph type --> e.g. shape (nElements, 3) = Triangles, shape (nElements, 4) = Quadrilaterals, etc. but just further food for thought. I would actually be very excited to see something like this go into Bokeh. But I do think that this would require very substantial up-front design planning and consideration. Specifically, things like `CDSView` and selection/inspection would not play nice with this as they currently stand. Basically all of the Bokeh "data model" of Bokeh accreted organically over the years. It would probably be a good idea to try and re-visit the way data is handled as a whole, in view of all the capabilities and requirements that are now known better than they were 12 years ago, but that might be getting into Bokeh 4.x territory. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14141","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14141/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14141/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14141/events","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14141","id":2636059155,"node_id":"I_kwDOADqB3M6dHxIT","number":14141,"title":"[FEATURE] Add a way to disable hovering for `Image` and `ImageRGBA`","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1310697832,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzEwNjk3ODMy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/TRIAGE","name":"TRIAGE","color":"e2e2e2","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-05T17:26:55Z","updated_at":"2024-11-06T00:06:51Z","closed_at":"2024-11-06T00:06:51Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem description

Currently (and to my knowledge), when you add a HoverTool to an `Image` or `ImageRGBA`, it will show a HoverTool for the data, even if there is no data. 

A simple example is the bottom left corner in this example will show `NaN` if you hover over it.  

``` python
import numpy as np
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, HoverTool
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show

N = 100
x = np.linspace(0, 10, N)
y = np.linspace(0, 10, N)
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(x, y)
image = np.sin(xx) * np.cos(yy)
image[: N // 2, : N // 2] = np.nan  # For these points no hover should be shown

source = ColumnDataSource(
    data={"image": [image], "x": [0], "y": [0], "dw": [10], "dh": [10]}
)
p = figure(x_range=(0, 10), y_range=(0, 10))
p.image(image="image", x="x", y="y", dw="dw", dh="dh", source=source)

hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[("Pixel Value", "@image")])
p.add_tools(hover)

show(p)
```

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/99bd357a-0537-4b88-b892-dd815f17972f)


### Feature description

Add a way to disable hover for specific ranges or a value for `Image` and `ImageRGBA`. 

### Potential alternatives

None, but I'm open to suggestions!

### Additional information

### Expected behavior

Tooltips should work when plot is in fullscreen.

### Observed behavior

Tooltips work fine on the plot, but when in fullscreen they don’t show up.

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show, output_notebook
output_notebook()

p = figure(tools='hover,fullscreen')

p.scatter([1, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3])

show(p)
```

(edit: and `TapTool`) should gain a new property:   
```
    hit_filter = Instance(SelectionFilter, help="""

    """)
```

A hit filter is a model with an `execute` method that accepts a `Selection` as well as a renderer model. To be used in the selection manager in the inspect codepath:
```
let hit_test_result = renderer_view.hit_test(geometry)
if (filter != null)
    hit_test_result = filter.execute(hit_test_result, renderer_view.model)
if (hit_test_result != null) {
......
``` 

The purpose of the hit filter is to be able to modify the initial raw hit test restult, i.e. by pruning `.indicies` down to only 1 result, etc. or it can return `null` if it wishes to cancel inspection results (no tooltips)

We will provide a `TopIndicesFilter` filter that prunes down to a specified N number of results, as well as a `CustomJSSelectionFilter` that users can implement arbitrary filtering with JS snippets. The idea being that it should accept the initial`fullset_indices` selection object and return a new one that actually gets used subsequently (e.g. with multiple indices pruned down to a single one, or with indices corresponding to nan values removed). 

The challenge, I think, is that `Selection` objects are nowadays actually quite complex:

```typescript
export namespace Selection {
  export type Attrs = p.AttrsOf<Props>

  export type Props = Model.Props & {
    indices: p.Property<OpaqueIndices>
    line_indices: p.Property<OpaqueIndices>
    multiline_indices: p.Property<MultiIndices>
    image_indices: p.Property<ImageIndex[]>
    view: p.Property<GlyphView | null>
    selected_glyphs: p.Property<Glyph[]>
  }
}
```

First off, we would need to commit to this structure, or else perhaps we could pass a subset of the fields individually to the user callback code. But mostly, it's just a lot to deal with. We will have to explain all these fields and what each of the their structures is, as well, as very clearly describe what must be returned. It's definitely come fussy and complicated input and output than say `CustomJSTransform` callback code has to deal with. Problem description

I was running through the docs and became confused on [this stage](https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/blob/branch-3.7/docs/bokeh/source/docs/first_steps/first_steps_2.rst#rendering-circles).

You tell us to "switch a line() to a circle()"... however, the code shows no mention of the `circle()` being used.  Instead, you use `scatter()`.

Clean up this area so it makes a bit more sense.  Either show us how to use `circle()` or show us how to use `scatter()`.

### Potential alternatives

Leave it as is.  Maybe it doesn't need to be changed?

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142/events","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142","id":2640226523,"node_id":"I_kwDOADqB3M6dXqjb","number":14142,"title":"[FEATURE] Make CodeRunner and python code loading configurable","user":{"login":"levsa","id":310653,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxMDY1Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/310653?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa","html_url":"https://github.com/levsa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/levsa/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1310697832,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzEwNjk3ODMy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/TRIAGE","name":"TRIAGE","color":"e2e2e2","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-07T08:14:37Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T17:29:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem description

When running tests, I want to use stubs or mocks when running the production code, and verify stuff afterwards. `DirectoryHandler` instantiating a `CodeRunner` which loads the production code makes this very hard, a workaround is to use `monkeypatch` and override with my own `CodeRunner` which monkeypatches stuff after loading.

### Feature description

A first step would be if one could choose the type of `CodeRunner` to be instansiated, or if it was created with a method in `DirectoryHandler` which could be overridden. Also, it would make test setup easier if `CodeRunner` called a custom `prepare` or `post_load` method in the `CodeRunner` class (empty in base class), that would make it easy to override and inject behavior.

I'm not sure what the next step would be, to make production code loaded by `CodeRunner` more testable. My current solution is to `monkeypatch` factory methods that create stubs instead of real services, in my own `CodeRunner.run` method.

### Potential alternatives

Another option might be to use an injector framework (like e.g. [injector](https://pypi.org/project/injector/) ) , where one can bind ones own kind of subclasses.

### Additional information

My code runner that can inject stubs in the loaded code:

```
    def database_service_stub():
        with Stub(DatabaseService) as service_stub:
            service_stub.get_users().returns([{'name': 'test_user'}])
        return service_stub

    def create_test_injector():
        injector = Injector()
        injector.binder.bind(DatabaseService, to=database_service_stub())
        return injector

    class MyCodeRunner(CodeRunner):
        def __init__(
            self,
            source: str,
            path: PathLike,
            argv: list[str],
            package: ModuleType | None = None,
        ) -> None:
            super().__init__(source, path, argv, package=package)

        def run(self, module: ModuleType, post_check: Callable[[], None] | None = None) -> None:
            super().run(module, functools.partial(self.test_setup, module, post_check))

        def test_setup(
            self, module: ModuleType, post_check: Callable[[], None] | None = None
        ) -> None:
            monkeypatch.setattr(module, 'create_injector', create_test_injector)
            post_check()
```

The code runner is monkeypatched like so (a bit ugly and easily broken):

```
        monkeypatch_session.setattr(
            bokeh.application.handlers.server_lifecycle, "CodeRunner", MyCodeRunner
        )

```


My loaded bokeh code would then have (`main.py`):

```
class BokehApp:
    @inject
    def __init__(self, document: Document, database_service: DatabaseService) -> None:
        self.document = document
        self.users = database_service.get_users()
     ...   

injector = create_injector()
injector.binder.bind(Document, to=curdoc())
injector.get(BokehApp)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2462834067","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142#issuecomment-2462834067","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14142","id":2462834067,"node_id":"IC_kwDOADqB3M6Sy92T","user":{"login":"bryevdv","id":1078448,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNzg0NDg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1078448?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv","html_url":"https://github.com/bryevdv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-07T17:29:53Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T17:29:53Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@levsa I don't have a strong sense of your use-case and requirements which is a disincentive for me to work on anything (risk of spending effort only to make the wrong thing). ### Expected behavior

On mobile, with `HoverTool` tooltip active, touching on any figure area should pop up the tooltip, regardless of zoom level (using mobile pinch-zoom). For example, on a mobile device, see:

https://www.snowpacktracker.com/btac/stormtracker

In previous Bokeh versions (v3.0.2) touching anywhere in the figure area would pop up the hover tool to see actual data values. The release of v3.6.1 enabled touch scrolling to work on mobile, but seems to have altered the tooltip behavior from previous versions.

### Observed behavior

The tooltip seems to work if you leave your finger on the mobile screen, without any zooming in. If you pinch-zoom in, it does not work at all. And if you just touch once, it does not stay active.

Problem description

It'd be nice if pandas was an optional dependency with polars as an alternative engine. It has the benefit of being faster than pandas for almost everything and it also would allow polars users to have smaller package sizes by not requiring pandas.

### Feature description

Have alternative methods that use polars instead of pandas. There could be a transition phase before all methods are bilingual where, if the input is a polars df but the polars version of the bokeh method hasn't been written yet then the user gets a warning that it's being converted to pandas to finish the task. If they don't have pandas installed they'd, of course, get an error. That warning might motivate more people to contribute to making those methods.	

### Potential alternatives

live with pandas conversions and dependencies :(

### Additional information

And it could probably be used in Bokeh in those places, e.g. where a user passed a dataframe as the `source` argument to a glyph function. 

The tricky part is that Bokeh sometimes also *converts* things to dataframes, in order to do work. Off the top of my head: `hexbin` and `legend_group` both need to do groupby operations. But they need to work on all inputs, things like numpy arrays or even just plain Python lists. Bokeh needs to be able to convert those inputs internally to *something concrete* in order to do the groupby operations. Currently that something is Pandas dataframes. Is there a mode where narwhals can accept *non-dataframe-things* and just choose a "backend" to use on them automatically? If not, I don't see how it can be useful for this subset of situations in Bokeh.     """)
```

A hit filter is a model with an `execute` method that accepts a `Selection` as well as a renderer model. To be used in the selection manager in the inspect codepath:
```
let hit_test_result = renderer_view.hit_test(geometry)
if (filter != null)
    hit_test_result = filter.execute(hit_test_result, renderer_view.model)
if (hit_test_result != null) {
......
``` 

The purpose of the hit filter is to be able to modify the initial raw hit test restult, i.e. by pruning `.indicies` down to only 1 result, etc. or it can return `null` if it wishes to cancel inspection results (no tooltips)

We will provide a `TopIndicesFilter` filter that prunes down to a specified N number of results, as well as a `CustomJSSelectionFilter` that users can implement arbitrary filtering with JS snippets. Problem description

It'd be nice if pandas was an optional dependency with polars as an alternative engine. It has the benefit of being faster than pandas for almost everything and it also would allow polars users to have smaller package sizes by not requiring pandas.

### Feature description

Have alternative methods that use polars instead of pandas. There could be a transition phase before all methods are bilingual where, if the input is a polars df but the polars version of the bokeh method hasn't been written yet then the user gets a warning that it's being converted to pandas to finish the task. If they don't have pandas installed they'd, of course, get an error. That warning might motivate more people to contribute to making those methods.	

### Potential alternatives

live with pandas conversions and dependencies :(

Enhance Bokeh Tooltips for Richer Data Context","user":{"login":"ChrisBSK","id":62293513,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMjkzNTEz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/62293513?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK","html_url":"https://github.com/ChrisBSK","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisBSK/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1310697832,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzEwNjk3ODMy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/TRIAGE","name":"TRIAGE","color":"e2e2e2","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-18T15:45:22Z","updated_at":"2024-11-18T16:19:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem description

Currently, Bokeh tooltips are limited to showing basic information about the data points (e.g., X and Y values). This works well for simple visualizations, but it can be insufficient when trying to provide more detailed or context-rich information. For example, in bar charts or other plots with categorical data, users often need more context such as percentages relative to the total, additional commentary, or other statistics to better understand the data.

In this following example, we would like to have more precise information on the points shown on the plot, than only the type of penguin:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/88c35a77-1957-4ba2-b0bd-cb04f45f661b)

Without a way to dynamically include additional information in the tooltip, users might miss out on key insights or have to rely on external sources or complex annotations outside the plot.

### Feature description

We propose enhancing the current tooltip functionality by allowing dynamic tooltips that display more comprehensive information. This feature would allow the tooltip to show:

-  **Basic information** such as X and Y values.

- **Derived statistics** such as the percentage of each value relative to the total.

- **Contextual comments or insights**, such as annotations or labels that describe the importance of a given data point.

The tooltip should update dynamically based on the data being hovered over, displaying additional context without cluttering the plot itself.

Additionally, this feature should allow for easy customization of the tooltip content, enabling users to specify what information they would like to show.


### Potential alternatives

I've been working on this and I have a proposition about some new arguments and their behavior. I will post a pull request so we can discuss if the solution is something of interest to the community

I've been working on this and I have a proposition about some new arguments and their behavior. I will post a pull request so we can discuss if the solution is something of interest to the community but to give you an intuition it will look like this

**Solution**

When the user hovers over a data point (such as a circle or a bar), the tooltip displays additional information like the exact value, as well as contextual information that changes based on the categories or the axis.

```python
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show
from bokeh.sampledata.penguins import data
from bokeh.transform import factor_cmap, factor_mark
from bokeh.models import HoverTool, ColumnDataSource
import pandas as pd

# Preprocessing for hover details
SPECIES = sorted(data.species.unique())
MARKERS = ['hex', 'circle_x', 'triangle']

# Calculate percentage of each species in the total population
species_counts = data['species'].value_counts()
data['species_percentage'] = data['species'].map(
    lambda x: f"{(species_counts[x] / len(data) * 100):.2f}%"
)

# Define adaptability based on body mass (in g)
data['adaptability'] = pd.cut(data['body_mass_g'], 
                              bins=[0, 3500, 4500, 6000], 
                              labels=["+", "++", "+++"])

source = ColumnDataSource(data)

# Create figure
p = figure(title="Penguin Size by Species", background_fill_color="#fafafa")
p.xaxis.axis_label = 'Flipper Length (mm)'
p.yaxis.axis_label = 'Body Mass (g)'

# Scatter plot with colors and markers
p.scatter("flipper_length_mm", "body_mass_g", source=source,
          legend_group="species", fill_alpha=0.4, size=12,
          marker=factor_mark('species', MARKERS, SPECIES),
          color=factor_cmap('species', 'Category10_3', SPECIES))

# Add hover tool
hover = HoverTool()
hover.tooltips = [
    ("Species", "@species"),
    ("Species Percentage", "@species_percentage"),
    ("Flipper Length", "@flipper_length_mm mm"),
    ("Body Mass", "@body_mass_g g"),
    ("Adaptability", "@adaptability")
]
p.add_tools(hover)

# Configure legend
p.legend.location = "top_left"
p.legend.title = "Species"

# Show plot
show(p)

```

### Additional information

- This feature would greatly enhance the usability of Bokeh for more complex data visualizations, such as financial or business intelligence dashboards, where context and insights are essential.

- A similar feature exists in libraries like **Plotly** and **Matplotlib**, where hover interactions display enriched information.

- This feature should work with existing Bokeh plotting capabilities, maintaining compatibility with other tools like legends, axis labels, and interaction controls.

- Customizable tooltips would also allow users to decide what information is most relevant for their visualizations, improving the adaptability of Bokeh.


It would be a pleasure to contribute to this feature if it's accepted, I really like the mindset of Bokeh's Community. 

@ChrisBSKK. ### Expected behavior

I found the subcoordinates_zoom.py example to show a degraded performance on Firefox compared to Chrome (see the recorded bits). When zooming in with the Wheel Zoom y-axis tool, the rendering appears to be much faster on Chrome. The difference is more marked when the number of points/traces is increased. For instance and on my machine, with `n_seconds = 30` (instead of `15`), I can still zoom in okay on Chrome while on Firefox it's close to unusable.

I originally observed this issue reviewing this HoloViz Multichannel Timeseries example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/418) that uses HoloViews with Bokeh and depends on this feature.

### Observed behavior

Chrome:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/335f2a5b-1c1d-4c7b-9d18-dd5e59920f08
Firefox:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/99642492-849f-49ba-b455-aeb73f8a58fc


### Example code

```Python
import numpy as np

from bokeh.core.properties import field
from bokeh.io import curdoc
from bokeh.layouts import column, row
from bokeh.models import (ColumnDataSource, CustomJS, Div, FactorRange,
                          GroupByModels, HoverTool, Range1d, Select,
                          Switch, WheelZoomTool, ZoomInTool, ZoomOutTool)
from bokeh.plotting import figure

# Generate sample data
n_channels = 100
n_seconds = 15

total_samples = 512 * n_seconds
time = np.linspace(0, n_seconds, total_samples)
data = np.random.randn(n_channels, total_samples).cumsum(axis=1)
channels = [f"EEG {i}" for i in range(n_channels)]

# Define hover tool
hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[
    ("Channel", "$name"),
    ("Time", "$x s"),
    ("Amplitude", "$y μV"),
])

# Define ranges
x_range = Range1d(start=time.min(), end=time.max())
y_range = FactorRange(factors=channels)

# Create the figure
p = figure(x_range=x_range, y_range=y_range, lod_threshold=None, tools="pan,reset,xcrosshair")

source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(time=time))
renderers = []

for i, channel in enumerate(channels):
    xy = p.subplot(
        x_source=p.x_range,
        y_source=Range1d(start=data[i].min(), end=data[i].max()),
        x_target=p.x_range,
        y_target=Range1d(start=i, end=i + 1),
    )

    source.data[channel] = data[i]
    line = xy.line(field("time"), field(channel), source=source, name=channel)
    renderers.append(line)

# Configure tools
level = 1
hit_test = False
only_hit = True

group_by = GroupByModels(groups=[renderers[0::2], renderers[1::2]])
behavior = "only_hit" if only_hit else group_by

ywheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(renderers=renderers, level=level, hit_test=hit_test, hit_test_mode="hline", hit_test_behavior=behavior, dimensions="height")
xwheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(renderers=renderers, level=level, hit_test=hit_test, hit_test_mode="hline", hit_test_behavior=behavior, dimensions="width")
zoom_in = ZoomInTool(renderers=renderers, level=level, dimensions="height")
zoom_out = ZoomOutTool(renderers=renderers, level=level, dimensions="height")

p.add_tools(ywheel_zoom, xwheel_zoom, zoom_in, zoom_out, hover)
p.toolbar.active_scroll = ywheel_zoom

# Add UI controls
level_switch = Switch(active=level == 1)
hit_test_switch = Switch(active=hit_test)
behavior_select = Select(
    disabled=not hit_test_switch.active,
    value=behavior,
    options=[
        ("only_hit", "Only hit renderers"),
        (group_by, "Even/Odd groups of renderers"),
    ],
)

# JavaScript callbacks
level_switch.js_on_change("active", CustomJS(
    args=dict(tools=[ywheel_zoom, zoom_in, zoom_out]),
    code="""
    const level = cb_obj.active ? 1 : 0;
    for (const tool of tools) {
        tool.level = level;
    }
    """
))

hit_test_switch.js_on_change("active", CustomJS(
    args=dict(tool=ywheel_zoom, select=behavior_select),
    code="""
    tool.hit_test = cb_obj.active;
    select.disabled = !cb_obj.active;
    """
))

behavior_select.js_on_change("value", CustomJS(
    args=dict(tool=ywheel_zoom),
    code="""
    tool.hit_test_behavior = cb_obj.value;
    """
))

# Layout
layout = column(
    row(Div(text="Enable zooming of sub-coordinates:"), level_switch),
    row(Div(text="Enable hit-testing based zooming:"), hit_test_switch),
    row(Div(text="Hit test behavior:"), behavior_select),
    p,
)

# Add layout to the document
curdoc().add_root(layout)
```

I do think passing the defs to individual `scatter` calls is a little awkward. At least personally, I'd rather register them explicitly up front to use later. I can imagine the same custom marker may want to be re-used and up-front registration fits that case better in my head. 
```python
custom_markers = {"@foo": ..., "@bar": ...}

Scatter.register(custom_markers)

plot.scatter(x, y, marker="@foo")
```

here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this approach?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14169/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14169/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2498813951","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/pull/14169#issuecomment-2498813951","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14169","id":2498813951,"node_id":"IC_kwDOADqB3M6U8N__","user":{"login":"bryevdv","id":1078448,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNzg0NDg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1078448?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv","html_url":"https://github.com/bryevdv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-25T19:03:09Z","updated_at":"2024-11-25T19:03:09Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@MarcoGorelli Thanks for the PR! * AFAIK the changes to `bokehjs/package-lock.json` should be reverted/dropped (cc @mattpap)

here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this here's my attempt at addressing #13780

- [x] issues: fixes #13780
- [x] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This allows both Polars DataFrames and PyArrow tables (and other eager libraries supported by Narwhals) to be passed to `ColumnSource`, e.g.
```python
data = (
    {
        "x_values": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        "y_values": [6, 7, 2, 3, 6],
        "colors": ["red", "green", "blue", "orange", "purple"],
        "sizes": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50],
    }
)
```

```python
import pyarrow as pa

tbl = pa.table(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=tbl)
```
and
```python
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame(data)
source = ColumnDataSource(data=data)
```

Would you be open to this All of Bokeh's glyphs, including `wedge` are implicitly "scatter" glyphs. So, you can scatter wedges of all different sizes on a plot to build up a chart exactly like the one you show. (In fact, there was an ancient example of *exactly* this type of chart in the repo more than ten years ago that was removed at some point, but I haven't been able to dig it up yet.) 

If you are asking for a dedicated API call just to make this kind of chart, I would say that is unlikely. Bokeh is a more foundational tool for general plotting and generally does not provide schematized charting APIs (that is left to higher level tools built on Bokeh). 

In any case if you have questions about how to create this chart using Bokeh APIs or want to discuss specifics of those details, that's a support discussion so it should move to the  [Discourse site](https://discourse.bokeh.org)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2501744634/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This is definitely a toy example. There's lots of ways to improve this using a single `wedge` call, using cumulative sum transforms to automatically compute the wedge angle offsets, using color mappers to color wedges dynamically based on other data, etc. Any of that can be discussed on Discourse. 
```
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show
import numpy as np

N = 5
PI2 = 2 * np.pi

red = np.random.random(N)
green = np.ones(N) - red
radius = (np.random.random(N) + 0.1) * 0.2

x = np.arange(N)
y = np.arange(N)

p = figure()
p.wedge(x=x, y=y, color="red", radius=radius, start_angle=0, end_angle=PI2*red)
p.wedge(x=x, y=y, color="green", radius=radius, start_angle=-PI2*green, end_angle=0)

show(p)
```

### Expected behavior

In the "Building applications" tutorial https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/user_guide/server/app.html there is a code example under the section "Updating from unlocked callbacks". Running this example using "bokeh serve --show APP_NAME" is supposed to create a Bokeh application which plots circles in random locations.

### Observed behavior

The Bokeh application created by running this example using "bokeh serve --show APP_NAME" does not plot any circles and produces the browser console error:  

[bokeh 3.6.0] attempted to retrieve bokeh.min.js?v=de958ab1870cd066dba119c34f40c5e1ca4af9c0d31988f4020e6c49a056838b8968f69e29f359fb6dae256aed026344b1797302c230126c662521f0895deb32:184 property array for nonexistent field 'radius'

### Example code

```Python
import asyncio
import time
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from functools import partial

from bokeh.document import without_document_lock
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource
from bokeh.plotting import curdoc, figure

source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=[0], y=[0], color=["blue"]))

i = 0

doc = curdoc()

executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2)

def blocking_task(i):
    time.sleep(1)
    return i

# the unlocked callback uses this locked callback to safely update
async def locked_update(i):
    source.stream(dict(x=[source.data['x'][-1]+1], y=[i], color=["blue"]))

# this unlocked callback will not prevent other session callbacks from
# executing while it is running
@without_document_lock
async def unlocked_task():
    global i
    i += 1
    res = await asyncio.wrap_future(executor.submit(blocking_task, i), loop=None)
    doc.add_next_tick_callback(partial(locked_update, i=res))

async def update():
    source.stream(dict(x=[source.data['x'][-1]+1], y=[i], color=["red"]))

p = figure(x_range=[0, 100], y_range=[0, 20])
l = p.circle(x='x', y='y', color='color', source=source)

doc.add_periodic_callback(unlocked_task, 1000)
doc.add_periodic_callback(update, 200)
doc.add_root(p)
```

Chrome Version 122.0.6261.69 (Official Build) (64-bit)

_No response_

### Expected behavior

when wheel zoom quickly in and out. plot.x_range should always have start < end

### Observed behavior

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8c30e961-1204-4494-aeb2-f879fc5dc331)
I was fiddling with the official example and noticed that, when wheel zoom quickly in and out, RangeUpdate event can have x0 > x1. and when it occurs, it will stay that x0 > x1 and for the plot, x_range has start > end. I noticed it applies to many official examples when zooming quickly in and out.

### Example code

```Python
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/3.0.0/docs/user_guide/interaction/js_callbacks.html#js-on-event-callback-triggers
```

There is no metadata in polar_subcoordinates.py or spans_and_strips.py located in examples/plotting/. To understand the purpose of these files, a new contributor would have to read through the code and try to understand what bokeh is doing when we could just present them with a detailed and in-depth explanation of the functionality. 

### Feature description

Add metadata to polar_subcoordinates.py and spans_and_strips.py.

### Potential alternatives

No alternatives but to add documentation unless unnecessary.

There is no metadata in polar_subcoordinates.py or spans_and_strips.py located in examples/plotting/. To understand the purpose of these files, a new contributor would have to read through the code and try to understand what bokeh is doing when we could just present them with a detailed and in-depth explanation of the functionality. 

### Feature description

Add metadata to polar_subcoordinates.py and spans_and_strips.py.

### Potential alternatives

No alternatives but to add documentation unless unnecessary.

Current test script:

``` python
from bokeh.models import FileInput, Button
from bokeh.io import curdoc

file_input = FileInput(directory=True)
button = Button(label="Clear file input")
button.on_click(lambda *e: setattr(file_input, "_clear_input",  file_input._clear_input + 1))

curdoc().add_root(file_input)
curdoc().add_root(button)
```
