{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699/events","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699","id":54311341,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1NDMxMTM0MQ==","number":1699,"title":"Displaying RGBA images from (N, M, 3-4) arrays","user":{"login":"imaluengo","id":1993103,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5OTMxMDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1993103?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo","html_url":"https://github.com/imaluengo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":91228996,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTIyODk5Ng==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/type:%20discussion","name":"type: discussion","color":"2659A0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2015-01-14T11:23:56Z","updated_at":"2024-10-16T19:03:48Z","closed_at":"2016-09-06T23:33:59Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is a suggestion. Currently the API of  `image_rgba` states:

> **bokeh.plotting.image_rgba(_args, *_*kwargs)**
> The image_rgba glyph takes each image as a two-dimensional array of RGBA values (encoded as 32-bit integers).
> **Parameters:**
> ...
> **image** (str or 2D array_like of uint32) – value or field names of RGBA image data

This is fine for mathematical plotting, where as the gallery examples show you can create a uint32 2D `(N, M)` array and `view` it as a `(N, M, 4)` uint8 array to assign values easily. However, if used for image processing or to show real images, they usually come in the form of `(N, M, 3-4)` arrays (whenever the alpha channel is included or not depends on the library used to load the image). 

The workaround for plotting the images is really simple, and it would allow `(N, M)` uint32 or `(N, M, 3-4)` uint8 images to be passed as an argument:

``` python
if img.ndim > 2: # could also be img.dtype == np.uint8
    if img.shape[2] == 3: # alpha channel not included
        img = np.dstack([img, np.ones(img.shape[:2], np.uint8) * 255])
    img = np.squeeze(img.view(np.uint32))
```

Do you think it might be useful to have the workaround inside the plotting function to allow faster plotting w/o needed to convert the image to uint32 everytime?

Additionally, it could be added support for other data type arrays (like `[0., 1.]` RGB float images).

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699/events","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699","id":54311341,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1NDMxMTM0MQ==","number":1699,"title":"Displaying RGBA images from (N, M, 3-4) arrays","user":{"login":"imaluengo","id":1993103,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5OTMxMDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1993103?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo","html_url":"https://github.com/imaluengo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/imaluengo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":91228996,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTIyODk5Ng==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/type:%20discussion","name":"type: discussion","color":"2659A0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2015-01-14T11:23:56Z","updated_at":"2024-10-16T22:51:55Z","closed_at":"2016-09-06T23:33:59Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is a suggestion. Currently the API of  `image_rgba` states:

> **bokeh.plotting.image_rgba(_args, *_*kwargs)**
> The image_rgba glyph takes each image as a two-dimensional array of RGBA values (encoded as 32-bit integers).
> **Parameters:**
> ...
> **image** (str or 2D array_like of uint32) – value or field names of RGBA image data

This is fine for mathematical plotting, where as the gallery examples show you can create a uint32 2D `(N, M)` array and `view` it as a `(N, M, 4)` uint8 array to assign values easily. However, if used for image processing or to show real images, they usually come in the form of `(N, M, 3-4)` arrays (whenever the alpha channel is included or not depends on the library used to load the image). 

The workaround for plotting the images is really simple, and it would allow `(N, M)` uint32 or `(N, M, 3-4)` uint8 images to be passed as an argument:

``` python
if img.ndim > 2: # could also be img.dtype == np.uint8
    if img.shape[2] == 3: # alpha channel not included
        img = np.dstack([img, np.ones(img.shape[:2], np.uint8) * 255])
    img = np.squeeze(img.view(np.uint32))
```

Do you think it might be useful to have the workaround inside the plotting function to allow faster plotting w/o needed to convert the image to uint32 everytime?

Additionally, it could be added support for other data type arrays (like `[0., 1.]` RGB float images).

PR adds a `destroy` method to Document, that removes several places where there might be circular references. However, this alone does not (yet) solve the problem, and a call to `gc.collect()` is required to fully release memory. A fuller solution down the road might be to implement a `destroy` protocol on all models so that they can be responsible for cleaning up any cycles.

- [x] issues: addresses #7468
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

A `StackColorMapper` for displaying RGB[A] images","user":{"login":"shaperilio","id":26465923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2NDY1OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26465923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio","html_url":"https://github.com/shaperilio","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1310697832,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzEwNjk3ODMy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/TRIAGE","name":"TRIAGE","color":"e2e2e2","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-17T15:07:13Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T15:38:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem description

In #1699 a desire was expressed to display RGBA images with shape (M, N, 3|4), e.g. directly out of `imread` (`matplotlib`). Using `image_rgba` works, but requires "bitpacking" (e.g. see [here](https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699#issuecomment-2417446913)) and then you can't get a hover tooltip with RGBA values out of the box (you just get the 32-bit value).

I explored the possibility of using `image_stack` instead, and it appears that the only thing missing is a `StackColorMapper` implementation that has an optional user-set minimum *and* maximum value.

The MRE below makes a plot with `WeightedStackColorMapper`, which does not produce a proper result; a weighted average makes an image with R, G, B = (1, 1, 1) show up as 33% grey. But it shows other things working: the hover tooltip shows the R, G, B tuple out of the box, and it would solve #1699 because no bit-packing is necessary to display a color image.

```python
import numpy

from bokeh.plotting import figure, output_file, show, save
from bokeh.models import WeightedStackColorMapper, LinearColorMapper
from bokeh.palettes import varying_alpha_palette

output_file('/tmp/plot.html)
w, h = 64, 64
p = figure(width=w * 16, height=h * 16,
           tools='hover', tooltips=[
               ('X, Y', '$x, $y'),
               ('R, G, B', '@image'),
           ])

# We need to define an alpha mapper but it doesn't actually matter:
# 1. The RGB values returned are ignored by the `WeightedStackColorMapper`.
# 2. The alpha values are unused because we have global alpha set to 1.0 (the
#    default).
alpha_mapper = LinearColorMapper(palette=varying_alpha_palette(color='white',
                                                               n=2,
                                                               start_alpha=0,
                                                               end_alpha=255))
cm = WeightedStackColorMapper(palette=['#FF0000', '#00FF00', '#0000FF'],
                              stack_labels=['R', 'G', 'B'],  # NB: don't know how to use these
                              alpha_mapper=alpha_mapper)


# *** test; should be white
R = numpy.ones((h, w), dtype=numpy.uint8)
G = numpy.ones((h, w), dtype=numpy.uint8)
B = numpy.ones((h, w), dtype=numpy.uint8)
image = [numpy.stack((R, G, B), axis=-1)]
p.image_stack(image=image,
              x=0, y=0,  # origin location of image
              dw=w, dh=h,  # extent of image
              color_mapper=cm,
              )
# *** ends up grey. `WeightedStackColorMapper` does a "weighted sum", i.e. for
# each pixel, the "total" = R+G+B+A, and each color is computed as color /
# total. So for all white, you get 1 / 3 = 33% grey.
show(p)
```

### Feature description

I propose a new `StackColorMapper` implementation; let's call it `RGBAStackColorMapper` for the purpose of discussion. If it were written in Python, it may look something like this:

```python
class RGBAStackColorMapper(StackColorMapper):
    def __init__(self,
                 min_color: float | None = None,
                 max_color: float | None = None,
                 palette: Sequence[str] | None = None
                 ) -> None:
        """
        Maps the values in a stack of shape (H, W, C) into RGBA values.

        Parameters
        ----------
        min_color: float | None
            Minimum value. If `None`, the minimum value in the stack is used.

        max_color: float | None
            Maximum value. If `None`, the maximum value in the stack is used.

        palette: Sequence[str]
            Sequence of colors, one for each channel in the stack. These colors
            represent the maximum value for that channel, i.e. the color
            displayed when a pixel has `max_color` for that channel. Must be
            length 3.
        """
        self._min_color = min_color
        self._max_color = max_color
        if palette is None:
            palette = ['#FF0000', '#00FF00', '#0000FF']
        self._palette = palette
        assert len(self._palette) == 3

    def map_to_RGBA(self, value: Sequence[float]) -> tuple[int, int, int, int]:
        """
        Maps a value to an RGBA value.

        Parameters
        ----------
        value: Sequence[float]
            The values of the stack at a particular pixel

        Returns
        -------
        Tuple[int, int, int, int]
            The RGBA value.
        """
        # Must have one color for each channel, possibly with alpha
        assert len(value) in (3, 4)

        # Clip values to the min and mx. (Assume `min_color` and `max_color`
        # have already been set to the stack's min and max if they were passed
        # in as `None`.)
        n = self._min_color
        x = self._max_color
        value = [min(max(v, n), x) for v in value]
        # TODO: special handling when x==n
        R = int((value[0] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        G = int((value[1] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        B = int((value[2] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        if len(value) == 4:
            A = int((value[3] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        else:
            A = 255
        return R, G, B, A
```

These are the features of this mapper:
1. `min_color` and `max_color` can be explicitly set (e.g. to 0 and 255 to show unaltered images loaded from disk). If not set, the min/max of the stack is used, which would have the effect of normalizing the image. The caller can also set explicit values (e.g. 64, 200) and values will be clipped (like the levels tool in Photoshop).
2. `palette` by default is simply RGB. Setting it allows other "colormaps" to be used.
3. There's no explicit setting of an alpha mapper. If the stack has 4 channels, the last one is assumed to be alpha. (This would require callers to shuffle their data around if it's not in RGBA channel order on the last dim, but I think that's minor.) The idea here is that, when working with `WeightedStackColorMapper` in the MRE above, I had to create an alpha mapper that was never used and thus could be basically anything, and I want to avoid that here.

For the MRE, the use would be as follows:
```python
cm = RGBAStackColorMapper(min_color=0, max_color=1)
```

### Potential alternatives

I could not reproduce the output of `image_rgba` with `image_stack` and `WeightedStackColorMapper`. I did not explore beyond that.

If the data is a single RGBA image then image_rgba is the glyph to use. If people are finding it difficult getting data in the format that image_rgba expects and/or to display the individual color components when hovering then I would rather add some extra helper functions to make these tasks easier.

Adding Python-side helper functions to assist with the necessary bit-packing was also my suggestion for a short-term improvement. That seems like and easy, self-contained, and reasonable addition in any case. 

That said, I can appreciate that users might legitimately want a direct option for multi-channel image specification as well: 

* Desire to modify images on a per-channel basis
* Desire to re-weight the image channels unequally
* Desire to provide images in non-RGBA channel formats
* Desire not avoid expensive data duplication in CDS to support interactive features

But maybe that points to a new multi-channel image glyph rather than an extension of public color mapper machinery. If so, that is not what I understood #11032 to be asking for. The ask there was some indication *before* starting the action, that the action is available at all, i.e. an aid to discoverability. 

That said, I think what is done here with cursors is all that is appropriate. The pointer is generally needed e.g. for tapping or hovering something, so I don't think the cursor should change to represent the "pan" option all the time just because a pan tool is enabled. So, I am not sure how best to close #11032 I think now that some non-cursor indication would need to be added to indicate that panning is available. PR adds a `destroy` method to Document, that removes several places where there might be circular references. However, this alone does not (yet) solve the problem, and a call to `gc.collect()` is required to fully release memory. A fuller solution down the road might be to implement a `destroy` protocol on all models so that they can be responsible for cleaning up any cycles.

- [x] issues: addresses #7468
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

adds a separate on demand bokehjs workflow for testing new versions of Chromium (issue #14090). This workflow is essentially equivalent to bokehjs' default workflow, but instead of running image baseline tests, this workflow only generates images, but doesn't compare them, because they would fail most of the time anyway. Note that this workflow may fail regardless, so inspection of the output is needed every time.

There is one optional argument to the workflow, which allows to specify the snap channel to install Chromium from. The default is `beta`. To determine what version is associated with each channel, use:
```sh
$ snap info chromium
(...)
channels:
  latest/stable:    129.0.6668.100 2024-10-11 (2972) 181MB -
  latest/candidate: 130.0.6723.44  2024-10-17 (2977) 180MB -
  latest/beta:      130.0.6723.44  2024-10-14 (2973) 181MB -
  latest/edge:      131.0.6753.0   2024-10-08 (2970) 184MB -
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14105/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14105/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2424260078","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/pull/14105#issuecomment-2424260078","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14105","id":2424260078,"node_id":"IC_kwDOADqB3M6Qf0Xu","user":{"login":"bryevdv","id":1078448,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNzg0NDg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1078448?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv","html_url":"https://github.com/bryevdv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-19T22:47:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-19T22:47:43Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Why not also run this weekly, say the night before the weekly meeting. accepted","color":"bfe5bf","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":18,"created_at":"2018-03-06T21:34:58Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T22:35:43Z","closed_at":"2018-03-12T17:08:43Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/pulls/7604","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/pull/7604","diff_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/pull/7604.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/pull/7604.patch","merged_at":"2018-03-12T17:08:43Z"},"body":"This PR adds a `destroy` method to Document, that removes several places where there might be circular references. However, this alone does not (yet) solve the problem, and a call to `gc.collect()` is required to fully release memory. A fuller solution down the road might be to implement a `destroy` protocol on all models so that they can be responsible for cleaning up any cycles.

- [x] issues: addresses #7468
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

Well let's take care of the easy one immediately: this is fully expected. The Bokeh server is asynchronous, but not multi-threaded. If you do expensive blocking work, it will block. There may be situation-specific strategies to adopt [1] but the general approach if blocking work cannot be avoided, especially on session startup, is to run multiple Boeh servers behind a load balancer [as described in the documentation](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/user_guide/server/deploy.html#load-balancing). This kind of horizontal-scaling is the explicit design goal for this scenario. 

[1] E.g. if every session just needs read-only access to the same giant data set then that expensive load could just happen once in a server-start callback, rather than on every single session creation. Or if the blocking work happens in a callback after the session load, users can delegate it to their own threads. PR adds a `destroy` method to Document, that removes several places where there might be circular references. However, this alone does not (yet) solve the problem, and a call to `gc.collect()` is required to fully release memory. A fuller solution down the road might be to implement a `destroy` protocol on all models so that they can be responsible for cleaning up any cycles.

@philippjfr I cannot repro *any* issue with pure-Bokeh code that allocates a huge array:: ``` import numpy as np from bokeh.plotting import figure, curdoc p = figure() p.scatter([1,2,3], [6,4,5]) a = np.arange(100_000_000) curdoc().add_root(p) ``` Opening up two tabs chews up 1.6 GB as seen in mem logs and in OSX activity monitor: But after closing both tabs and waiting for the session cleanup to happen, all of the relevant ojbects are all gone: Note that these stats are reported directly out looking through all of the output of `gc.get_objects()` so if they did exist there is no way they could not show up. HOWEVER, with the code above, there _are_ objects left ofter: What I would very much like to see in a new issue is a reproducer that that does not use holoview or hvplot because until there is some evidence otherwise, it is my suspicion that any problems may come from there. PR adds a `destroy` method to Document, that removes several places where there might be circular references. However, this alone does not (yet) solve the problem, and a call to `gc.collect()` is required to fully release memory. A fuller solution down the road might be to implement a `destroy` protocol on all models so that they can be responsible for cleaning up any cycles. - [x] issues: addresses #7468 - [ ] tests added / passed - [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change) ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/7604/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/7604/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2429712720","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/pull/7604#issuecomment-2429712720","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/7604","id":2429712720,"node_id":"IC_kwDOADqB3M6Q0nlQ","user":{"login":"bryevdv","id":1078448,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNzg0NDg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1078448?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv","html_url":"https://github.com/bryevdv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-22T16:16:28Z","updated_at":"2024-10-22T16:16:28Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@epifanio yes, again, for the memory issue there should be a new fresh issue. That said, I also cannot reproduce anything with the new code either: * 3 tabs open, activity monitor reports 543MB in use: * tabs closed, after session cull: activity monitor reports memory gone done, Bokeh reports all objects released: I am still skeptical that there is actually an issue with Bokeh itself, rather than something else. Here's where you can add some additional logging for some of the types you are using: https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/blob/949d7956f9caaeb78f47d37db2a2f2894bb10afd/src/bokeh/server/tornado.py#L745 i.e. add code there to print out any un-reclaimed xarrays or or numpy arrays or netcdf objects (like the current code does with pandas dataframes) and then open and close tabs, and then *wait for the closed sessions to be culled* and see if there are any unexpected objects left. If there are not, then that means Python itself is telling us that there are no objects of that type, and the memory number that is concerning you is just wrong or else has a different interpretation than expected. If there are unexpected objects left, then that is something to investigate, but any issue still might possibly lie either way those libraries rather than Bokeh itself. This is the kind of info that should go in the new issue. goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design.

The effects of this PR will be:

* better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes)
* better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case)
* support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools
* ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design.

The effects of this PR will be:

* better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes)
* better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case)
* support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools
* ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

[Screencast from 16.08.2024 11:49:27.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6503c161-8640-4477-a352-4b1673e99d1e)

goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design. The effects of this PR will be: * better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes) * better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case) * support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools * ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools) * anything related to accessibility [Screencast from 16.08.2024 11:49:27.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6503c161-8640-4477-a352-4b1673e99d1e) - * ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design.

The effects of this PR will be:

* better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes)
* better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case)
* support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools
* ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

[Screencast from 16.08.2024 11:49:27.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6503c161-8640-4477-a352-4b1673e99d1e)

- [x] fixes #13566 (regression test added)
- [x] addresses #10205
- [x] fixes #6662 goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design.

The effects of this PR will be:

* better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes)
* better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case)
* support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools
* ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

[Screencast from 16.08.2024 11:49:27.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6503c161-8640-4477-a352-4b1673e99d1e)

- [x] addresses #10205
- [x] fixes #6662 goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design.

The effects of this PR will be:

* better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes)
* better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case)
* support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools
* ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

[Screencast from 16.08.2024 11:49:27.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6503c161-8640-4477-a352-4b1673e99d1e)

- [x] fixes #13566 (regression test added)
- [x] addresses #10205
- [x] fixes #6662 Python version      :  3.9.16 (main, Mar  8 2023, 10:39:24) [MSC v.1916 64 bit (AMD64)]
IPython version     :  8.12.0
Tornado version     :  6.2
Bokeh version       :  3.2.0
BokehJS static path :  <omitted>
node.js version     :  (not installed)
npm version         :  (not installed)
Operating system    :  Windows-10-10.0.22631-SP0

### Browser name and version

Firefox 131.0.3

### Expected behavior

Given an object created as figure, when inserting an image into it using `image_url` and explicitely stating a width and height, it would be expected that the image is stretched to match these.

### Observed behavior

While this works perfectly fine for pixel formats such as PNG, this does not work with SVG. While these can be resized, this can be done only in a 1:1 ratio wrt the screen space.

Below is a screenshot of the observed behaviour. Note that while the screenshot was made within Jupyter Notebook, the bug also persists when using Bokeh Server instead.

![screenshot](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a740a4d2-9600-4541-aa40-0180ce72801a)

### Example code

The following code requires the two images test.png and test.svg from above.
Alternatively, any pair of images which are an SVG and it's exported pixel image will do.
The images above were created as a green square of which it is easy to see whether it gets stretched or not. For the SVG, I made sure that the viewing area matches the square.

```Python

from bokeh.plotting import figure, show
from bokeh.io import output_notebook 

output_notebook()

plot = figure(x_range=(0,100), y_range=(0,100))
plot.background_fill_color = "red"

plot.image_url(url=['test.png'], x=25, y=25, w=25, h=10)

plot.image_url(url=['test.svg'], x=75, y=75, w=25, h=10)

show(plot)
```

https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/discussions/14114

Same as the one used on HoloViews [holoviz/holoviews@main/.github/workflows/lock.yaml](https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/blob/main/.github/workflows/lock.yaml)

It is set to lock 100 issues/PRs every hour, every day. When the backlog is completed, it can be changed to run each Tuesday night (the commented-out cron job).

Example:

PR adds a `destroy` method to Document, that removes several places where there might be circular references. However, this alone does not (yet) solve the problem, and a call to `gc.collect()` is required to fully release memory. A fuller solution down the road might be to implement a `destroy` protocol on all models so that they can be responsible for cleaning up any cycles.

goal of this PR is allow both canvas and DOM/CSS rendering for renderers for which it makes sense, i.e. renderers that are UI components and renderers that include text. Secondary goal is to replace custom layouts with CSS layout where every possible without a major redesign. I expect only a selected subset of renderers will be covered in this PR. I started with `Legend` because it touches every aspect that I need to cover by the new design.

The effects of this PR will be:

* better integration with DOM/CSS (e.g. responsiveness to font loading/changes)
* better theming and customizability beyond what visuals offer (no support for save/copy tool in this case)
* support for text selection without the need for any custom implementations and new tools
* ease of development (renderers are inspectable in devtools)
* anything related to accessibility

[Screencast from 16.08.2024 11:49:27.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6503c161-8640-4477-a352-4b1673e99d1e)

- [x] addresses #10299 
- [x] fixes #13566 (regression test added)
- [x] addresses #10205
- [x] fixes #6662 - [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are +    const [range] = this.ranges
+    const {start, end} = range
+    const {log10} = Math
+    switch (this.dimension) {
+      case 0: {
+        const {x0, width} = this.bbox
+        return log10(end/start) * (sx - x0) / width + log10(start)
+      }

PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are +class AxisClick(ModelEvent):
+    ''' Announce a location where an axis was clicked.
+
+    '''
+    event_name = 'axis_click'
+
+    value: float | FactorType | None
+
+    def __init__(self, model: Axis | None, value: float | FactorType | None = None) -> None:
+        from .models import Axis
+        if model is not None and not isinstance(model, Axis):
+            clsname = self.__class__.__name__
+            raise ValueError(f"{clsname} event only applies to axis models")
+        super().__init__(model=model)

PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are +

PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are Problem description

Creating this request as a follow up from this discourse: [link](https://discourse.bokeh.org/t/adding-decoration-to-multiline/11772?u=prateekarya)

I am trying to add decorations to `multi_line`, specifically arrowheads. Here is the code snippet:

```
from bokeh.models import VeeHead
ml = p.multi_line(xs='xs', ys='ys', color='black', source=dataSource)
ml.add_decoration(VeeHead(size=10), "end")
```

The code does not throw any exceptions, but it does not draw arrowheads. So, I am guessing the functionality exists but it's not implemented yet.

### Feature description

Enable decorating `multi_line` with Vee and Tee heads.

### Potential alternatives

Use more `multi_line` to draw these decoations.

### Additional information

```
from bokeh.models import VeeHead

docs","color":"D0CBB8","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/milestones/75","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/milestone/75","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/milestones/75/labels","id":8077366,"node_id":"MI_kwDOADqB3M4Ae0A2","number":75,"title":"3.x","description":"","creator":{"login":"mattpap","id":27475,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/27475?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap","html_url":"https://github.com/mattpap","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":201,"closed_issues":1,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-06-13T18:11:11Z","updated_at":"2024-08-27T20:48:58Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":11,"created_at":"2024-04-07T17:54:06Z","updated_at":"2024-09-07T04:04:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"(This issue mostly pertains to the contents of `bokeh.models`)

Our reference docs are currently hand-curated into topical groupings. The original reason for the the groupings was that early on we had issues supporting and effective search capability, and because the API surface was much smaller and easier to organize by hand. Search has since gotten better and the API surface has grown considerably.

In addition to posing a manual maintenance burden, this organization also results in som very heavy pages in our docs. These large page sizes can cause problems or block new features:

* https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13799
* https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/12707

I think we must split the API docs into a one-class-per-page organization. I.e. instead of:

```
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/models/tools.html#bokeh.models.TapTool
```

there would be a dedicated page like:
```
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/models/TapTool
```

This organization is common in other projects and is supported by some tools e.g. [`autoapi`](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) and others. It will take some research and experimentation to see whether these tools can be used "out of the box" or if we will need to make other changes to accommodate them or (worst-case) develop our our tooling. I'll try to do some minimal investigation to see how/if existing tools currently function.

There are some questions that probably intersect with this, such as the idea to split up the monolithic `bokeh.models` namespace itself at the user-facing API level (cc @mattpap I thought there was an issue/discussion about this but I can't find it)

cc @bokeh/dev for comments (not triaging as "discussion" since I think the necessity of change is making itself evident)
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13814/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13814/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2335030157","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13814#issuecomment-2335030157","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13814","id":2335030157,"node_id":"IC_kwDOADqB3M6LLbuN","user":{"login":"bryevdv","id":1078448,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNzg0NDg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1078448?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv","html_url":"https://github.com/bryevdv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bryevdv/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-07T04:04:33Z","updated_at":"2024-09-07T04:04:33Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> cut down the massive amount of information is not just one-page-per-class but also to remove redundant / inherited information.

Just to be clear the reference docs did not originally duplicate inherited information. It was changed to the current form explicitly, and on purpose, because years ago, some users *strenuously, **vociferously*** complained that not having every entry be "complete" meant that it should not even properly be called a reference. So. Truly, there is absolutely no satisfying everyone. 

Yes, I personally also think there is too much information. But there is *alot* of factoring in order to optimize the cross-runtime implementation, and not all of that factoring will be useful, relevant, or obvious to users. In other worse, I think just deleting `:inherited-members:` and call it a day will absolutely result in too *little* information on many entries, without question (even with links to base classes). 

So, I don't know what a good solution is here.

     def event_values(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
         return dict(**super().event_values(), model=self.model)
 
+class AxisClick(ModelEvent):
+    ''' Announce a location where an axis was clicked.
+
+    '''
+    event_name = 'axis_click'
+
+    value: float | FactorType | None
+
+    def __init__(self, model: Axis | None, value: float | FactorType | None = None) -> None:
+        from .models import Axis
+        if model is not None and not isinstance(model, Axis):
+            clsname = self.__class__.__name__
+            raise ValueError(f"{clsname} event only applies to axis models")
+        super().__init__(model=model)

PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are docs","color":"D0CBB8","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/milestones/75","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/milestone/75","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/milestones/75/labels","id":8077366,"node_id":"MI_kwDOADqB3M4Ae0A2","number":75,"title":"3.x","description":"","creator":{"login":"mattpap","id":27475,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3NDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/27475?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap","html_url":"https://github.com/mattpap","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattpap/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":201,"closed_issues":1,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-06-13T18:11:11Z","updated_at":"2024-08-27T20:48:58Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":13,"created_at":"2024-04-07T17:54:06Z","updated_at":"2024-09-14T15:11:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"(This issue mostly pertains to the contents of `bokeh.models`)

Our reference docs are currently hand-curated into topical groupings. The original reason for the the groupings was that early on we had issues supporting and effective search capability, and because the API surface was much smaller and easier to organize by hand. Search has since gotten better and the API surface has grown considerably.

In addition to posing a manual maintenance burden, this organization also results in som very heavy pages in our docs. These large page sizes can cause problems or block new features:

* https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13799
* https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/12707

I think we must split the API docs into a one-class-per-page organization. I.e. instead of:

```
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/models/tools.html#bokeh.models.TapTool
```

there would be a dedicated page like:
```
https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/models/TapTool
```

This organization is common in other projects and is supported by some tools e.g. [`autoapi`](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) and others. It will take some research and experimentation to see whether these tools can be used "out of the box" or if we will need to make other changes to accommodate them or (worst-case) develop our our tooling. I'll try to do some minimal investigation to see how/if existing tools currently function.

There are some questions that probably intersect with this, such as the idea to split up the monolithic `bokeh.models` namespace itself at the user-facing API level (cc @mattpap I thought there was an issue/discussion about this but I can't find it)

cc @bokeh/dev for comments (not triaging as "discussion" since I think the necessity of change is making itself evident)

Maybe an issue should be raised somewhere else regarding this?

Not at all. The intent of leaving off `:inherited-members:` is to leave off inherited members. It does exactly what it says. My first comment is that we would need to do some work to circumvent the obvious stated behavior for out special case, and my second comment was that that may in fact already be the case in our extensions. 

> I do think inherited member should at the very least be treated differently. Even a simple fix like a collapsible (which is already done for the JSON prototypes) would do wonders, like:

Putting things in a hidden, collapsible state makes linking directly a problem. I use direct anchor links in answers to support questions, if not daily, then at least several times a week. I am a big :-1: on this approach. 

I think we should not get off track here. Going to one page per class will be a huge improvement. That itself will also be somewhat of a lift to achieve, so it's all I plan to expend my own efforts on (when I have the time).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2351025900/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall.

describe("AxisClick event", () => {
  it("shoud support linear axes", async () => {
    const plot = new Plot({
      x_range: new Range1d({start: 0, end: 10}),
      y_range: new Range1d({start: 0, end: 10}),
    })
    const axis = new LinearAxis()
    plot.add_layout(axis, "below")

    const events: AxisClick[] = []
    axis.on_event(AxisClick, (event: any) => events.push(event))

    const {view} = await display(plot)

    const actions = new PlotActions(view, {units: "screen"})
    await actions.tap(xy(100, 5)) // should be near the middle of the x-axis
    await paint()

    expect(events.length).to.be.equal(1)
    expect(events[0].value).to.be.equal(10)
  })
})

PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [ ] tests added / passed
- [ ] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are This makes it so RC and full releases will be published to the bokeh conda channel under the label `rc`. 

I don't have the power to create the rc label, but this needs to be done before this PR is merged. PR adds support for an `AxisClick` / `"axis_click"` event that reports the location that an axis was clicked. 

- [x] issues: fixes #601
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall.

from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource,HoverTool x = np.linspace(0, 10, 300) y = np.linspace(0, 10, 300) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(x, y) d = np.sin(xx) * np.cos(yy) s1 = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=[xx], y=[yy], image=[d])) p = figure(width=400, height=400) p.x_range.range_padding = p.y_range.range_padding = 0 p.image(image='image',source=s1, x=0, y=0, dw=10, dh=10, palette="Sunset11", level="image") hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[ ("index", "$index"), ("(x,y)", "($x, $y)"), ("Temperature", "@image"),], mode='mouse',) p.add_tools(hover) show(p) ``` ![Screenshot_20240907_042606](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9ebf2149-2a40-4553-97c5-4ed5d90b34f4) ### Observed behavior However, the custom tooltip for @image shows all the data instead of a specific number. x: $x
y: $y
image: @image
""", )) show(p) ``` ### Stack traceback or browser console output The issue reported initially here: https://discourse.bokeh.org/t/custom-hover-tooltip-over-image-plot-not-work-as-expected/11802. Software versions Python version 3.11.4| Bokeh version 3.5.2 ### Browser name and version _No response_ ### Jupyter notebook / Jupyter Lab version _No response_ ### Expected behavior When I hover over an image plot (heatmap) using custom tooltip, I expect to get a tooltip like that I get with the code below (without custom tooltip): ``` import numpy as np from bokeh.plotting import figure, show from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource,HoverTool x = np.linspace(0, 10, 300) y = np.linspace(0, 10, 300) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(x, y) d = np.sin(xx) * np.cos(yy) s1 = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=[xx], y=[yy], image=[d])) p = figure(width=400, height=400) p.x_range.range_padding = p.y_range.range_padding = 0 p.image(image='image',source=s1, x=0, y=0, dw=10, dh=10, palette="Sunset11", level="image") hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[ ("index", "$index"), ("(x,y)", "($x, $y)"), ("Temperature", "@image"),], mode='mouse',) p.add_tools(hover) show(p) ``` ![Screenshot_20240907_042606](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9ebf2149-2a40-4553-97c5-4ed5d90b34f4) ### Observed behavior However, the custom tooltip for @image shows all the data instead of a specific number. The code with the custom tooltip is below. ![Screenshot_20240907_042305](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1db3c914-6d81-446e-90ef-7cb6f317ed8a) ### Example code ```Python import numpy as np from bokeh.plotting import figure, show from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource,HoverTool x = np.linspace(0, 10, 300) y = np.linspace(0, 10, 300) xx, yy = np.meshgrid(x, y) d = np.sin(xx) * np.cos(yy) s1 = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=[xx], y=[yy], image=[d])) p = figure(width=400, height=400) p.x_range.range_padding = p.y_range.range_padding = 0 p.image(image='image',source=s1, x=0, y=0, dw=10, dh=10, palette="Sunset11", level="image") p.add_tools(HoverTool(tooltips="""
x: $x
y: $y
image: @image
""", )) show(p) ``` ### Stack traceback or browser console output The issue reported initially here: https://discourse.bokeh.org/t/custom-hover-tooltip-over-image-plot-not-work-as-expected/11802. I would appreciate it if you could give me any idea how to increase the font size of the default tooltip. ### Screenshots _No - [x] issues: fixes #601
- [x] tests added / passed
- [x] release document entry (if new feature or API change)

This can be tested by adding code like the following to nearly any example":
```python
from bokeh.models import CustomJS
p.xaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))

p.yaxis[0].js_on_event("axis_click", CustomJS(code="""
    alert('It works! ' + cb_obj.value)
"""))
```

This needs some polish:

* @mattpap as usual any suggestions for BokehJS tests welcome. I did not see much existing tests for these sorts of events that I might look to emulate
* @bokeh/dev thoughts on interesting examples? This has been long-requested but there were never concrete use-cases described that I recall. 
* @tcmetzger any thoughts on where / how much narrative docs to add are https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/13921

This PR improves positioning of `ScaleBar` by allowing mixed screen and data coordinates.

Example:

[Screencast from 30.07.2024 16:07:10.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/508b24d8-03f8-428b-a860-d8f76544f835)

<details>
  <summary>Code</summary>

```py
import numpy as np
from bokeh.core.properties import field
from bokeh.io import show
from bokeh.layouts import column
from bokeh.models import (ColumnDataSource, HoverTool, Range1d, ScaleBar, FactorRange, Metric, WheelZoomTool)
from bokeh.palettes import Category10
from bokeh.plotting import figure

n_eeg_channels = 7
n_pos_channels = 3
n_channels = n_eeg_channels + n_pos_channels
n_seconds = 15
total_samples = 512 * n_seconds
time = np.linspace(0, n_seconds, total_samples)
data = np.random.randn(n_channels, total_samples).cumsum(axis=1)
channels = [f"EEG {i}" for i in range(n_eeg_channels)] + [f"POS {i}" for i in range(n_pos_channels)]

hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[
    ("Channel", "$name"),
    ("Time", "$x s"),
    ("Amplitude", "$y"),
])

x_range = Range1d(start=time.min(), end=time.max())
y_range = FactorRange(factors=channels)

p = figure(x_range=x_range, y_range=y_range, lod_threshold=None, tools=["pan","reset",hover])

source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(time=time))
renderers = []

for i, channel in enumerate(channels):
    subp = p.subplot(
        x_source=p.x_range,
        #y_source=y_target_range,
        y_source=Range1d(start=data[i].min(), end=data[i].max()),
        x_target=p.x_range,
        y_target=Range1d(start=i, end=i + 1),
    )

    source.data[channel] = data[i]
    line = subp.line(field("time"), field(channel), color=Category10[10][i], source=source, name=channel)
    renderers.append(line)

from bokeh.models.annotations.legends import Val
k_channel = 4
scale_bar = ScaleBar(
    range=renderers[k_channel].coordinates.y_source,
    unit="mm",
    dimensional=Metric(base_unit="mm"),
    orientation="vertical",
    location=("left", Val(f"EEG {k_channel}")),
    anchor="center_left",
    label_location="right",
    background_fill_color=None,
    border_line_color=None,
    bar_length=1.0,
    bar_length_units="data",
    margin=10, # default margin and padding make positioning awkward in the data setup
    padding=0,
    length_sizing="exact",
)
p.add_layout(scale_bar)

ywheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(renderers=renderers, level=1, dimensions="height")
p.add_tools(ywheel_zoom)
p.toolbar.active_scroll = ywheel_zoom

show(column(p))
```
